steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: Isotopes of an element have…
options: The same mass number but different atomic numbers
The same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
The same number of neutrons but different numbers of protons
Identical numbers of protons, neutrons and electrons
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Isotopes always have the same atomic number (same number of protons — same element), but a different number of neutrons, giving them different mass numbers.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Isotopes have the same number of protons (same element) but different numbers of neutrons, so they have different mass numbers.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.4.1 — Atoms and isotopes

steps.title: Isotopes
steps.bodyHTML: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the same number of protons (same atomic number Z) but a different number of neutrons, giving them different mass numbers. For example, carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both carbon (Z = 6) but have 6 and 8 neutrons respectively. Some isotopes are unstable (radioactive) and will decay over time.
commonErrors: Thinking isotopes are different elements — they are not; same proton number means same element
Confusing mass number with atomic number
Forgetting that isotopes of the same element have the same chemical properties

aqa.questionText: Uranium-235 and uranium-238 are isotopes. Uranium has atomic number 92. State how these two isotopes are similar and how they are different.

steps.label: State the similarity
steps.content: Both isotopes have the same atomic number, so they have the same number of protons:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — same number of protons (or same atomic number)

steps.label: State the difference
steps.content:
They have different mass numbers, so different numbers of neutrons:
U-235: neutrons = 235 − 92 = 143
U-238: neutrons = 238 − 92 = 146
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — different number of neutrons (or different mass numbers)

aqa.finalAnswer: Same number of protons (92); different numbers of neutrons (143 and 146)

aqa.questionText: Which of the following pairs are isotopes of the same element?

options.label: A
options.text: An atom with 6 protons and 6 neutrons; an atom with 7 protons and 6 neutrons

options.label: B
options.text: An atom with 8 protons and 8 neutrons; an atom with 8 protons and 10 neutrons

options.label: C
options.text: An atom with 6 protons and 6 neutrons; an atom with 6 protons and 6 neutrons

options.label: D
options.text: An atom with 17 protons and 18 neutrons; an atom with 18 protons and 17 neutrons

markSchemeSteps: Isotopes must have the same number of protons
Option B: both have 8 protons but different neutron numbers (8 and 10) ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option C has identical atoms (not isotopes). Option A and D have different proton numbers, so they are different elements.
